2026-05-05.title: Colordle #1502 Answer: Royal Blue (#4169E1)
2026-05-05.summary: Today's answer is Royal Blue, a vivid medium-dark blue that sits right in the sweet spot of the spectrum. It's not navy, not sky, but that classic primary punch. If you burned guesses on purple or teal, you missed the mark on saturation. Here is how to lock it down next time.
bonusHints: Think of the blue in a standard RGB color wheel, not the deep ocean.
This hex code has significantly more green than a pure violet would.
The name implies a specific historical pigment, not just any blue.
Check the distance from pure black; it is much brighter than you might assume.
2026-05-05.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

Royal Blue (#4169E1) hit us on May 5, 2026, for puzzle number 1502. It is a bold, saturated choice that demands you commit to the blue family early. Unlike yesterday's Plum, which dragged us into muddy purples, or the day before's Slate that hid in the grays, this one screams primary color. If you started with anything desaturated, the feedback loop probably sent you spinning. The hex code breaks down to 65 red, 105 green, and 225 blue. That high blue value paired with a solid green chunk is what keeps it from turning violet. It is a clean, digital-friendly blue that shows up often in UI design, so it should feel familiar. Do not overthink the name; it is literally royal blue, not a trick shade like periwinkle or azure.

What kind of color this is

Royal Blue lives in the blue hue family, but it sits in a very specific pocket of saturation and brightness. It is darker than a sky blue but nowhere near the abyss of a navy. Visually, it pops. When you look at the RGB values, the blue channel dominates heavily, but the red and green are not zeroed out, which gives it body. Compare this to Vantablack from May 1st, which absorbed almost all light values. Royal Blue reflects a ton of energy. It is the kind of color you use for a call-to-action button because it draws the eye without being aggressive like pure red. In the context of recent answers, it feels like a reset after the muted tones of Slate and the organic green of Mint. It is unapologetically synthetic and vibrant.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

Start with a pure blue anchor. If your first guess is something like #0000FF, you get a baseline for maximum blue. From there, look at the distance metrics. If the game tells you the answer is darker, you might lean toward navy. If it says lighter, go sky. For Royal Blue, you need to darken that pure blue slightly and add a touch of red and green to mute the electric feel just enough. A common mistake is staying too cold. Royal Blue has warmth compared to an icy cyan. Try guessing a standard medium blue first, then adjust saturation. If your guess feels too gray, pump up the saturation. If it feels too purple, drop the red. The key is balancing the green channel; too little green and you drift into violet territory like Plum. Too much green and you hit teal. Keep the green moderate, around the 100 mark on the 0-255 scale.

Where players usually go wrong

The biggest trap with Royal Blue is confusing it with navy or standard blue. Players see the word "Royal" and assume it must be dark and moody, so they guess deep indigos. Then the feedback says "lighter," and they panic, jumping straight to baby blue. They miss the middle ground entirely. Another frequent error is oversaturating the red channel. Because the name sounds fancy, people expect purple undertones. They bump up the red value, thinking it adds richness, but that just turns the guess into a violet or periwinkle. Royal Blue is distinctly blue, not purple-adjacent. Also, do not ignore the hex code patterns. If you are guessing #4060E0 and getting close feedback, do not jump to #202080. Stay in the same brightness neighborhood. The error margin on the blue channel is small, but the red and green channels are where the specific identity lives. Most failed runs I see involve wild swings in brightness rather than fine-tuning the hue mix.

How the score logic actually helps

The scoring algorithm in Colordle rewards proximity in all three RGB channels, but it weighs them differently depending on the target. For a color like Royal Blue, the blue channel is your anchor. If your blue value is off by 50 points, no amount of tweaking red and green will save the guess. The logic highlights which channel is driving the distance. If you guess a color with the correct blue value but wrong red/green, the score improves significantly, telling you the brightness is right but the hue is off. This is your cue to stop moving the brightness slider and start mixing. Conversely, if the blue channel is wrong, the score stays low regardless of how pretty your purple or teal guess looks. Use the numerical feedback to isolate the outlier channel. If red and green distances are small but blue is huge, you know exactly which slider to grab. Do not spray and pray; adjust one variable at a time based on which channel distance shrinks.

Questions players keep asking

Why does Royal Blue feel different from Navy?

Navy blue has a much lower lightness value and often less saturation. It leans heavily into the darks, sometimes almost black. Royal Blue maintains high saturation and a mid-range brightness. It is energetic, whereas Navy is reserved. In hex terms, Navy often has values under 100 for all channels, while Royal Blue pushes the blue channel over 200.

Is this considered a warm or cool blue?

It is a cool color by definition, but within the blue family, it leans slightly warmer than an electric blue due to the red content. It lacks the greenish tint of a cyan, which makes it feel more traditional. It is not "ice" blue; it has substance.

How often do primary-adjacent colors appear?

More often than you think. After a run of obscure shades like Slate or Plum, the game often resets with a recognizable standard. Royal Blue is a staple. If you see a string of weird, muddy colors, expect a primary or near-primary color to follow as a palate cleanser.

Can I guess this without knowing the name?

Absolutely. The name is flavor text. The hex code and visual position matter. If you treat it purely as a coordinate in 3D color space (RGB), the name is irrelevant. Focus on the ratio of red to green to blue. A 1:1.5:3.5 ratio roughly gets you into this territory. Ignore the monarchy references and look at the numbers.

2026-05-04.title: Colordle 1501 Answer: Plum (#66386A) - May 4, 2026
2026-05-04.summary: Today's Colordle answer is Plum, a deep, muted purple with hex code #66386A. After yesterday's cool Slate, we're diving back into rich tones that sit right on the edge of neutral. If you burned guesses on bright magentas or leaned too hard into blue-violets, this one stung. Here is how to lock down this specific shade next time.
bonusHints: Think less electric purple and more dried flower.
The red channel is lower than you might expect for a 'purple'.
It sits darker than standard web purple but lacks the blackness of Vantablack.
Check the balance between blue and red; they are nearly even.
2026-05-04.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

We are looking at Plum for May 4, 2026. The hex code is #66386A. This is day 1501, and frankly, it is a tricky one if you came in expecting something vibrant. We just had Vantablack two days ago and Slate yesterday, so the jump to a color with actual chroma felt jarring for some. But Plum isn't your standard-issue purple. It is dusty, muted, and sits heavily in the mid-tones.

If you guessed bright orchid or neon violet, the game told you immediately that you were way off on saturation. This color lives in the shadows. It is the kind of shade you find in old upholstery or overripe fruit left on the counter. The specific hex values tell the real story: Red is 102, Green is 56, and Blue is 106. Notice how close the red and blue values are? That near-equality is what kills the brightness and gives it that distinctively flat, plum-like appearance.

What kind of color this is

Plum (#66386A) is a desaturated dark violet. Calling it just "purple" feels lazy because it lacks the punch of a primary purple. It leans slightly more blue than red, but only by a hair. In the HSL spectrum, this thing is sitting around 298 degrees hue, which is standard purple territory, but the saturation is where it gets interesting. It is barely hanging on to about 30% saturation. That is why it feels so heavy.

Compare this to the Vermilion from April 30th. Vermilion screams for attention with high saturation and brightness. Plum whispers. It absorbs light rather than reflecting it. The lightness value hovers around 24%, making it significantly darker than a medium purple but not quite black. It occupies that uncomfortable middle ground where colors often lose their identity and just look "muddy" if you aren't careful with your guesses. It is a sophisticated, if somewhat depressing, color.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

Start by establishing the floor. Since we had Vantablack recently, your brain might be wired to think "dark" means "no color." Do not make that mistake. Plum has color, just very little light. Your first guess should test the water temperature between blue and red. Try a standard medium purple like #800080. If the game tells you the hue is close but the brightness is wrong, do not swing wildly to pink or indigo.

Instead, drag your slider down into the grays. The key to finding #66386A is reducing saturation before you adjust the hue. Most players overshoot on the red channel because they associate "plum" with redder fruits. But this specific hex code is cool-toned. Once you lock in the purple-ish hue, stop touching the hue slider. Focus entirely on the brightness and saturation knobs. You need to dull it down. Add gray. Make it muddy. If your guess looks like a crayon, you are still too bright. Keep desaturating until it looks like a shadow.

Where players usually go wrong

The biggest trap here is the name association. People hear "Plum" and think of the skin of a ripe Santa Rosa plum, which can be surprisingly bright and pinkish-red. Or they think of plum juice, which is deep but vibrant. The web color "Plum" is actually a light lavender, which is the exact opposite of today's answer. If you aimed for any version of light plum, you wasted a guess.

Another common error is ignoring the green channel. In a color this dark, the green value (56) is significant. It is not zero. If you try to force a pure blue-red mix with no green, you end up with a digital purple that looks synthetic. Real-world dark purples almost always have enough green in them to kill the vibrancy. Players who kept their green channel at zero or near-zero kept getting feedback that their color was too "clean" or "electric." You need that dirt in the mix.

How the score logic actually helps

Colordle's distance metric is brutal on low-saturation colors because the perceptual difference between "close" and "way off" shrinks. When you are dealing with a color like #66386A, moving the slider by 5 points can feel like a massive jump in the score. Use that. If your score drops significantly when you lower the brightness by a tiny increment, you know you are in the right neighborhood.

Also, look at the hue shift. If you are at 290 degrees and the score suggests you are far, do not just spin the wheel to 300. Check if the issue is actually saturation. Often, a high distance score on a muted color means your saturation is too high, not that your hue is wrong. The algorithm penalizes high saturation errors heavily on dark colors because they stand out more against the target. Treat the score as a saturation gauge first, hue gauge second.

Questions players keep asking

Why does Plum look different on my screen?

Because #66386A is so close to gray, monitor calibration plays havoc with it. On a high-contrast OLED, this might look like a rich eggplant. On a washed-out laptop screen, it might just look like a dirty gray. Do not trust your eyes blindly today. Trust the numbers. If the RGB values are roughly equal with blue slightly dominant, you are there, even if it looks brownish to you.

Is this considered a warm or cool color?

It is technically cool because of the blue dominance, but it is so desaturated that it acts neutral. It does not have enough heat to be warm, despite the red component. In a design context, you would use this as a neutral, similar to how you used Slate yesterday, not as an accent color.

How does this compare to yesterday's Slate?

Slate (#516572) was blue-green dominant and felt industrial. Plum (#66386A) is red-blue dominant and feels organic, albeit decaying organic matter. Both share that low-light, high-gravity feel. If you nailed Slate by keeping things muted, you should have been able to pivot to Plum by just shifting the hue toward red and killing the green.

What is the best next guess if I missed today?

Try a color with the same lightness but higher saturation to see which direction the hue shifts. Or, pick a color like Indigo (#4B0082) to test the lower bound of the purple spectrum. You need to triangulate the muddiness. Do not guess a bright color next; stay in the dark, muted lane to refine your understanding of the game's current preference for heavy tones.

2026-04-29.title: Colordle Answer for April 29, 2026 - Cool Blue #4984B8
2026-04-29.summary: Day 1496 brings us Cool Blue, a steady blue with green undertones that sits comfortably in the mid-range saturation. If you've been tracking recent colors, you'll notice we've bounced from earth tones to primaries and back. This one rewards players who think in hue families rather than chasing specific shades.
bonusHints: The green undertone is subtle but real—don't lock yourself into pure blue guesses if you've already tried those.
Saturation-wise, you're looking at something neither muted nor electric. Mid-range territory.
Check yesterday's Spelunking—if you noted how far that dark blue-green was from pure blue, this one sits roughly opposite on the saturation scale.
Recent history shows a pattern: after the wild card that was Purple (#800080), we're back to something more predictable. Lean into that.
2026-04-29.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

April 29, 2026. Day 1496. The answer is Cool Blue, hex code #4984B8. This one falls squarely in the blue hue family but carries enough green in its mix to keep players who guess purely cyan or navy guessing. The saturation sits at a comfortable mid-range—vibrant enough to register clearly, muted enough that it won't punch you in the face like a neon.

If you've been playing daily, you know the last few days have been a ride. Yesterday gave us Spelunking, that moody dark blue-green. Before that, the actual purple (#800080) showed up and threw off anyone who doesn't see purple as a primary color. Sunshine and Kiwi rounded out the week with warm tones. Now we're back to blue territory, but not the deep ocean kind. Think of a clear sky on a temperate day—not the intense summer blue, but the reliable blue that shows up most often.

The hex code breaks down as 49 red, 84 green, and B8 (184) blue. That green value being higher than red tells you something. It's not a blue with purple leanings. It's a blue that wants to be friendly, approachable, almost teal-adjacent without crossing over. Players who understand hex composition will spot the green undertone immediately. Those relying on pure color memory might need an extra guess or two.

What kind of color this is

Cool Blue is exactly what it sounds like—a blue that doesn't try too hard. It's the color of a default link on a well-designed website, the blue in corporate logos that want to seem trustworthy without being boring, the sky most stock photos use when they need something that reads as optimistic but professional.

In design terms, this sits in the "accessible blue" category. It has enough contrast against white and dark backgrounds to be readable without being aggressive. Web designers pick colors like this when they need something that won't alienate anyone. It's the blue of buttons that say "Continue" or "Submit." Not exciting, but effective.

The hue angle for #4984B8 puts it around 210 degrees on the color wheel, which is classic blue territory. What pushes it away from pure blue is the saturation and lightness. At roughly 50% saturation and 55% lightness, it's neither washed out nor deeply saturated. Think of it as the blue equivalent of a medium-voiced person in a meeting—present, clear, and easy to understand.

What makes this color interesting is its versatility. It works as a primary color in palettes, as an accent against warm backgrounds, or as a neutral-adjacent option when you need something less aggressive than royal blue but more defined than powder blue. Players who think in terms of use cases will recognize this as a "safe but competent" blue—the kind designers reach for when they need universal appeal.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

Start with any blue in the mid-saturation range. Colors like steel blue, cornflower, or your standard medium blue will all return useful information. The game will tell you how close you are through its scoring system, which we'll get into later, but the real narrowing happens when you test the green undertone.

If you guess a pure blue—something like #0000FF or a standard medium blue without green leanings—you'll see that the green component is off. That feedback tells you to head toward cyan or teal territory. But don't overshoot into full cyan (#00FFFF) because that swings the saturation too far. You're looking for something that sits between cyan and blue, closer to blue than cyan, but definitely not pure blue.

Another approach: test a few colors in the blue family with varying lightness. Start with a lighter blue to establish the lightness floor, then go darker to find the ceiling. Since this color sits at medium lightness, you'll find it sandwiched between pastel blues and navy blues. Once you've bracketed the lightness, you can fine-tune the hue.

The hex code structure gives you another angle. The red value (49) is significantly lower than green (84) and blue (184). If you guess something like #4477CC, you're in the right ballpark but might be slightly too saturated or too purple-leaning. Adjust from there based on feedback. Each guess should eliminate a dimension—whether that's hue, saturation, or lightness—so you're converging on #4984B8 within three to four educated guesses if you're reading the clues well.

Where players usually go wrong

The biggest mistake with this particular color is assuming "blue" means "pure blue." Players who immediately guess #0000FF or a standard medium blue without checking the hex composition often waste a guess getting the saturation wrong. They see blue, they guess blue, and then they're confused when the score doesn't match expectations.

Another common error: overcorrecting toward cyan. When players realize this blue has green in it, some swing too far and guess something like #00B8D4 or a full cyan. That sends them down the wrong branch of the color tree. The green undertone is subtle—present but not dominant. You want a blue that contains green, not a green that contains blue.

Players also struggle with the lightness. This isn't a light blue (think sky blue, powder blue) and it's not a dark blue (think navy, midnight). It's the blue of a business casual outfit—present but not loud. People who anchor on lighter blues as their mental reference for "soft blue" will keep guessing too light. Those who anchor on dark blues for "deep blue" will keep guessing too dark. The sweet spot is in the middle.

Finally, there's the recent colors trap. Seeing Purple (#800080) yesterday makes some players gun for colors with purple in them today. Don't. Each day's answer is independent. The purple was a one-off, a reminder that the game can throw curveballs. Today is back to straightforward territory. Cool Blue is the opposite of a trap—it's exactly what it claims to be.

How the score logic actually helps

The Colordle scoring system gives you three pieces of information for each guess: exact match, partial match, and miss. Understanding how to read these together is what separates quick solvers from those who burn through guesses without making progress.

For this color, an exact match means you've nailed the hue, saturation, and lightness for that particular channel. A partial match means you're in the right neighborhood but off on the specifics. The system doesn't just tell you "you're wrong"—it tells you how you're wrong, which is the actual advantage of playing a logic-based color game rather than a pure guessing game.

When you guess a color and see that the blue channel is exact but green is only partial, you know to preserve the blue value while adjusting green. If you guessed #4488CC and got exact on red, partial on green, and partial on blue, you'd know that red is locked (or very close), green needs movement in the right direction, and blue is in the ballpark but not perfect. That feedback should tell you to bump green up slightly and adjust blue based on whether you're too light or too saturated.

The scoring also helps you avoid wasted guesses on obviously wrong branches. If you've guessed three blues and none of them returned any exact matches, you might be in the wrong hue family entirely. But since we know this is blue, the logic should guide you toward the correct saturation and lightness rather than cycling through unrelated hues.

Think of it as a three-dimensional coordinate system. Each guess narrows your position in that space. The goal isn't to guess the exact color in one shot—that's nearly impossible. The goal is to converge on it through logical elimination, using the score feedback as your map. Players who treat each guess as a data point rather than a hope tend to solve in fewer attempts.

Questions players keep asking

Is Cool Blue the same as Steel Blue or Cornflower Blue?

Similar, but not identical. Steel Blue (#4682B4) runs slightly darker and more saturated. Cornflower Blue (#6495ED) is lighter and more saturated with a hint more purple. Cool Blue sits between them in lightness but closer to Steel Blue in saturation. If you guessed either of those, you'd be in the right hue family and reasonably close on saturation, but you'd likely need one more adjustment to nail the exact hex. The game would show you partial matches pointing toward the specific differences.

Why does this color feel harder than yesterday's Spelunking?

Spelunking (#35465E) was dark and desaturated—a narrow slice of color space that's relatively distinctive. Cool Blue sits in a crowded neighborhood. There are dozens of blues in the medium saturation, medium lightness range. The closer colors are to each other, the harder they are to distinguish, which means you need more precision in your guesses. With Spelunking, you could be off by a few hex values and still get useful feedback. With Cool Blue, you need to be more precise because nearby blues will return similar scores until you hit the right one.

Should I check the recent colors before guessing?

It helps, but not in the way you might think. Recent colors don't influence today's answer—the game generates each day's color independently. However, looking at recent colors helps you calibrate your monitor and your color perception. If you've been playing on a screen that runs warm, you might perceive this blue as slightly green when it's actually neutral. Seeing the exact hex values of recent colors and comparing them to what you saw gives you a baseline for how your display handles blues versus yellows versus greens. Use history for calibration, not prediction.

What's the best first guess for this color?

Something in the mid-range blue family that you know well. A standard medium blue like #4169E1 (Royal Blue) or #4682B4 (Steel Blue) works well as a baseline. You want a guess that gives you clean data—not a color so far off that everything comes back as misses, but not so close that you get lucky on the first try without learning anything. A baseline guess establishes your starting position in color space. From there, the feedback tells you which direction to move. If Royal Blue returns partial matches on green and blue, you know to adjust those channels. If it returns mostly misses, you're further off than expected and need to make bigger jumps.

Does the hex code structure give any shortcuts?

Yes. The hex format breaks into RGB channels, and you can use that structure to make systematic adjustments. Red is at 49 (low), green at 84 (medium), blue at 184 (high). If you're guessing and see that your green is too high, you know to pull it back. If your blue is too low, push it up. Each channel is independent, so you can solve each one separately and combine them. Players who think in hex rather than color names tend to solve faster because they're working with numbers rather than subjective descriptions like "too purple" or "not saturated enough."
